questions.question: Which statement best describes palliative care?
options: Care that focuses on comfort and quality of life for the whole person living with a serious, incurable illness, and which can begin well before the final days
Care aimed at curing the underlying illness as quickly as possible
Care given only in the final few hours of life, once all other options have stopped
Care that concentrates only on treating physical symptoms and pain

questions.question: How is end of life care best described in relation to palliative care?
options: A completely separate service that has nothing to do with palliative care
Care that begins only after a person has already died
The part of palliative care given when a person is thought to be in the last year, and especially the last days, of their life
Care given years before any serious illness develops

questions.question: When care is described as 'holistic', what does this mean?
options: It focuses only on the person's medicines and physical symptoms
It looks after the whole person and those close to them, including emotional, social and spiritual needs
It replaces the judgement of the nursing and palliative care team
It is delivered entirely by a single care worker

questions.question: What is the main part of a care worker's role in end of life care?
options: To decide alone when a person is dying and inform the family
To change the person's medicines whenever symptoms appear
To diagnose the person's illness and decide which treatments they need
To support everyday comfort and dignity, follow the care plan, and report changes to a nurse or senior

questions.question: Who delivers end of life care?
options: Only the person's family members
A single care worker acting on their own
Only doctors, with no involvement from anyone else
A multidisciplinary team, such as district nurses, GPs, specialist palliative care nurses and sometimes hospice staff

questions.question: Which of the following is a change commonly seen as a person enters the last days of life?
options: A sudden, lasting increase in appetite and energy
Wanting to eat and drink much more than usual
Becoming very drowsy, sleeping more or being hard to rouse
Breathing that becomes deeper and more regular than normal

questions.question: Which national guidance describes changes that are commonly seen as death approaches?
options: A DNACPR form
The Gold Standards Framework alone
The Mental Capacity Act
NICE NG31

questions.question: What is the main purpose of advance care planning?
options: To speed up paperwork so staff can spend less time with families
To make a legally binding demand for any treatment the person chooses
To let the care team decide in advance what is best without involving the person
To let a person set out their wishes and preferences for future care while they are able, so these can guide decisions if they can no longer speak for themselves

questions.question: How does the Gold Standards Framework help teams?
options: It helps teams identify people who may be approaching the end of life early, assess their needs and wishes, and plan their care
It removes the need to review a person's plan as things change
It allows care workers to prescribe medicines themselves
It requires that all care decisions are made in a crisis rather than in advance

questions.question: What is a ReSPECT plan?
options: A plan that covers resuscitation and nothing else
A Recommended Summary Plan for Emergency Care and Treatment that sets out, in the person's own words alongside clinical advice, what should and should not happen in an emergency
A record used only after a person has died
A form that withdraws all comfort and pain relief from a person

questions.question: What does a DNACPR decision mean?
options: That the person will no longer receive food, fluids or pain relief
That if the person's heart or breathing stops, cardiopulmonary resuscitation will not be attempted
That all comfort and dignity measures should stop
That the care worker may decide alone whether to attempt CPR

questions.question: How is a DNACPR decision made and recorded?
options: By the care worker who spends the most time with the person
It is made informally and never written down
By the family alone, with no clinical involvement
It is a clinical decision, recorded on a form, and should be discussed with the person or those close to them

questions.question: How does the Mental Capacity Act begin when considering a person's ability to make a decision?
options: It gives the care team the right to decide for the person straight away
It assumes everyone can make their own decisions, and says a person must be given all reasonable help to do so before anyone decides they cannot
It assumes a person cannot make any decisions once they are seriously ill
It treats capacity as fixed and unchanging once assessed

questions.question: Which statement about mental capacity is correct?
options: Capacity is the same for every decision a person faces
Capacity is decision-specific and can change from day to day
Once a person loses capacity for one decision, they lose a voice in all decisions
Capacity can only ever be assessed by a family member

questions.question: When a person can no longer make a particular decision, how must any decision made for them be reached?
options: By ignoring any valid advance decision the person made
By choosing whatever is quickest for the care team
In the person's best interests, taking account of their past wishes, feelings, beliefs and values
By excluding anyone holding lasting power of attorney for health

questions.question: A person who is very drowsy and cannot speak may still be in pain. Which of these can be a sign of that pain?
options: Sleeping peacefully with no change in expression
Asking clearly and directly for pain relief
Appearing relaxed and settled during personal care
A furrowed brow, tense or rigid limbs, groaning, or becoming distressed during movement

questions.question: Who gives prescribed medicines, including 'as required' pain relief, to a person at the end of life?
options: Any family member who is present
The appropriate professional, such as the nurse
The care worker, using another resident's medicines
The care worker, adjusting the dose as they see fit

questions.question: Why does frequent mouth care matter so much at the very end of life?
options: Because it is a way of forcing the person to take fluids
Because a dry mouth is a common cause of distress
Because it speeds up recovery from the illness
Because it replaces the need for any other comfort care

questions.question: Why is it natural for someone to want little or no food and drink as they come to the very end of life?
options: Because they no longer need any comfort or company
Because they are deliberately refusing to cooperate
Because they should always be encouraged to eat more to build strength
Because it is part of the body slowing down, not the person 'giving up'

questions.question: What is often the most valuable emotional support you can offer someone facing the end of life?
options: Finding the perfect words to take away their fear
Telling them exactly what will happen and when
Reassuring them that they have nothing to be sad about
Simply being there, to sit, listen and let them feel they are not alone

questions.question: How should you approach a person's cultural, religious and spiritual needs at the end of life?
options: Never assume; ask respectfully, follow the care plan, and support the person's and family's wishes
Arrange customs yourself without telling your senior
Decide that spirituality only ever means organised religion
Assume their needs from their background so you do not have to ask

questions.question: A person appears unconscious in their last hours. How should you communicate around them?
options: Stop talking to them, since they cannot hear anything now
Speak as loudly as possible to try to wake them
Continue to speak gently, tell them what you are doing and keep including them, because hearing is often the last sense to fade
Talk about their condition freely at the bedside as though they were not there

questions.question: A family member asks you a question about the person's illness and what to expect. What is the best response?
options: Answer what you can honestly within your role, and pass questions about the illness or treatment to the nurse
Give them a confident answer even if you are unsure, to reassure them
Tell them it is not your job to speak with families
Promise them an outcome so they feel better

questions.question: Which of these is good practice in caring for a person after death?
options: Working as quickly as possible and skipping the person's cultural or religious wishes
Deciding that the family does not need time with the person
Beginning washing or last offices straight away, before checking faith customs or the family's wishes
Continuing to treat the person with the same gentleness, privacy and dignity as in life, and following any recorded cultural or religious wishes

questions.question: How should a care worker regard their own feelings of grief when someone they have cared for dies?
options: As something that never happens to professional carers
As normal and valid, and looking after their own wellbeing is not a weakness but helps them keep caring for others
As a sign of weakness that should be hidden from colleagues
As a reason to stop working in care altogether

questions.question: Palliative care can begin months or even years before the final days, running alongside other treatment.
options: True
False

questions.question: Recognising that a person is dying is a decision a care worker should make alone and then tell the family.
options: True
False

questions.question: A DNACPR decision relates only to CPR and does not mean that comfort, pain relief, food, fluids or dignity should stop.
options: True
False

questions.question: Forcing food or fluids on someone at the very end of life is a good way to build up their strength.
options: True
False

questions.question: Because hearing is often the last sense to fade, you should keep speaking gently to a dying person and tell them what you are doing.
options: True
False

questions.question: Under the Mental Capacity Act you should assume a person cannot make their own decisions until it is proven that they can.
options: True
False

questions.question: Care and respect for the person continue after death, including following any recorded cultural or religious customs.
options: True
False

questions.question: A care worker may change a person's medicines themselves if they believe the person is in pain.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are features of good, holistic palliative care? (Select all that apply)
options: Attending to emotional, social and spiritual needs as well as physical ones
Caring for the mind and spirit, not only the body
Aiming to cure the underlying illness as quickly as possible
Helping people live as well as possible, focusing on quality of life

questions.question: Which of these can be signs that a person may be entering the last days of life? (Select all that apply)
options: Sleeping more, becoming very drowsy or hard to rouse
Wanting to take up vigorous exercise
Eating and drinking less, losing interest in food and fluids
Changes in breathing, such as irregular patterns or noisy breaths
A sudden, lasting increase in appetite and physical strength

questions.question: Which of the following are among the five priorities for the care of a dying person? (Select all that apply)
options: Recognise that the person may be dying and act on it
Prescribe and adjust medicines yourself as a care worker
Involve the person and those important to them in decisions
Hasten the person's death
Communicate clearly and sensitively

questions.question: Which of these are part of a care worker's role in end of life care? (Select all that apply)
options: Decide alone about the person's treatment
Diagnose the person's illness
Notice changes in pain, mood, eating or breathing
Help with comfort, cleanliness and gentle company
Pass concerns on promptly so the team can act

questions.question: Which of the following support everyday comfort and dignity at the end of life? (Select all that apply)
options: Repositioning gently to ease pressure and aid breathing
Keeping the room bright and noisy to keep the person awake
Keeping the mouth and lips moist and clean
Encouraging large meals even when the person cannot manage them
Keeping the person clean, warm and their surroundings calm

questions.question: Which of the following may form part of a person's cultural, religious or spiritual needs at the end of life? (Select all that apply)
options: Prayers, rituals or particular foods
Wishes about who may be present
Assuming their needs from their background rather than asking respectfully
A meaning found in relationships, nature or a life's story rather than religion
Ignoring the family's wishes to save time

questions.question: Which of these help a care worker look after their own and colleagues' wellbeing when someone dies? (Select all that apply)
options: Taking your breaks and using the support that is offered
Ignoring a colleague who is clearly struggling
Allowing yourself to feel and to grieve
Hiding your feelings because grief is a sign of weakness
Sharing how you feel with colleagues or your manager

questions.question: A decision not to attempt resuscitation is recorded on a ___ form.
___ is often the last sense to fade, so you should keep speaking gently to a dying person.
A ___ plan (Recommended Summary Plan for Emergency Care and Treatment) sets out what should and should not happen if a person suddenly becomes very unwell.
Care that looks after the whole person and those close to them, not only the body, is described as ___ care.
Care after death is sometimes called last ___.
